content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Learn English with Momo",
  "game_name": "Momo's Word Quest",
  "genre": "Educational Puzzle",
  "core_mechanics": "The game operates as a series of distinct mini-game challenges focused on English vocabulary. Player actions are primarily driven by keyboard input (typing, number key selection) and mouse clicks (for menu navigation and virtual keyboard interaction). Each game session (level) consists of a fixed number of questions from a specific vocabulary set and mini-game type. Correct answers increment the player's score and 'correct streak' counter. Incorrect answers decrement a 'mistakes allowed' counter (if applicable). A timer runs for each question, and potentially for the entire session. If the player runs out of time or makes too many mistakes, the level ends in failure. Completing a level successfully unlocks the next level. A 'hint' mechanism can be triggered, reducing the player's score but assisting with the current question. State tracking includes: `currentLevel`, `currentQuestionIndex`, `score`, `correctAnswersCount`, `incorrectAnswersCount`, `timeLeftForQuestion`, `totalTimeForLevel`, `currentMiniGameType`, `currentVocabularySet`, `currentWordData` (word, image, definition, options).",
  "visual_elements": "The game will feature a clean, cartoonish aesthetic. Momo, the friendly mascot, will be prominently displayed on the screen, likely as a static sprite with a subtle idle animation (e.g., blinking). \n\n**UI Elements:**\n*   **Background:** Simple, engaging themed backgrounds for each level/menu.\n*   **Momo:** Central character sprite, approximately 20% of screen height, positioned to the side of the main gameplay area.\n*   **Score Display:** Top-right corner, clearly readable text: 'SCORE: [Current Score]'.\n*   **Level Indicator:** Top-left corner, clearly readable text: 'LEVEL: [Current Level Number]'.\n*   **Timer:** A countdown bar or numerical display near the top-center, indicating `timeLeftForQuestion` or `totalTimeForLevel`.\n*   **Question Area:** A central rectangular panel displaying the current word, image, or definition based on the mini-game type.\n*   **Answer Options:** \n    *   **Matching Game:** 4-6 image or text buttons arranged in a grid below the question area. \n    *   **Multiple Choice:** 4 text buttons, each labeled with a number (1-4) for keyboard selection, arranged vertically below the question.\n*   **Virtual Keyboard (for Typing Game):** A grid of rectangular buttons representing a QWERTY keyboard layout, positioned at the bottom of the screen. Includes A-Z, Space, Backspace, Enter.\n*   **Hint Button:** A small 'Hint' button (or 'H') on the UI.\n*   **Feedback:** Visual (e.g., green/red overlay on answer, subtle particle effect for correct) and auditory cues for correct/incorrect answers.\n*   **Level Transition Screen:** A full-screen overlay displaying 'LEVEL COMPLETE!' or 'LEVEL FAILED!', along with score summary and a 'Next Level' or 'Main Menu' button.\n\n**Animations/Effects:**\n*   Button hover/click animations (slight scale, color change).\n*   Correct answer feedback: Green flash, brief 'star' particle effect, score value floats up.\n*   Incorrect answer feedback: Red flash, 'X' icon appears.\n*   Momo blinking/slight breathing animation.\n*   Typing feedback: Keys on virtual keyboard highlight when pressed.",
  "control_scheme": "The game primarily uses keyboard input for gameplay and mouse clicks for menu navigation and virtual keyboard interaction. \n\n*   **ArrowLeft / ArrowRight / ArrowUp / ArrowDown:**\n    *   **Menu Navigation:** Select options in Main Menu, Level Select.\n    *   **Matching/Multiple Choice:** Highlight different answer options (a visual box around the selected option).\n*   **Space / Enter:**\n    *   **Confirm Selection:** Select the currently highlighted menu or answer option.\n    *   **Submit Answer:** In Typing games, submit the typed word.\n    *   **Advance:** On Level Complete/Failed screens, proceed.\n*   **Keys '1', '2', '3', '4':**\n    *   **Direct Selection:** In Multiple Choice mini-games, directly select the corresponding option without needing to navigate first.\n*   **Keys 'A'-'Z', 'Backspace', 'Shift':**\n    *   **Typing Game:** Input characters into the text field. 'Shift' for capitalization.\n*   **Mouse Click:**\n    *   **General UI Interaction:** Clicking buttons in menus, hint button, virtual keyboard keys, answer options (as an alternative to keyboard selection).\n\nThere are no special key combinations required beyond Shift for capitalization in typing.",
  "game_states": "The game will transition through several distinct states:\n\n1.  **MainMenu:**\n    *   Displays 'Play', 'High Scores', 'Options' buttons.\n    *   **Transitions From:** LevelComplete, EndGame, Options, HighScores.\n    *   **Transitions To:** LevelSelect (via 'Play'), HighScores (via 'High Scores' button), Options (via 'Options' button).\n2.  **LevelSelect:**\n    *   Displays buttons for unlocked levels (e.g., 'Level 1', 'Level 2').\n    *   **Transitions From:** MainMenu, LevelComplete.\n    *   **Transitions To:** GamePlaying (via level button click).\n3.  **GamePlaying:** (Sub-states for internal flow)\n    *   **QuestionIntro:** Display the new word/image, task instructions briefly. (e.g., 'Match the word...')\n    *   **AwaitingInput:** Player sees the question and options, timer is running, player inputs answer.\n    *   **Feedback:** Brief display of correct/incorrect status for the answer.\n    *   **QuestionTransition:** Short pause before the next question or level end.\n    *   **Transitions From:** LevelSelect, Paused.\n    *   **Transitions To:** LevelComplete (on success), EndGame (on failure), Paused.\n4.  **LevelComplete:**\n    *   Displays 'LEVEL COMPLETE!', final score for the level, 'Total Score', and 'Next Level' / 'Main Menu' buttons.\n    *   **Transitions From:** GamePlaying (successfully completed all questions within parameters).\n    *   **Transitions To:** LevelSelect (via 'Next Level' or 'Main Menu' button).\n5.  **EndGame:**\n    *   Displays 'LEVEL FAILED!' (or 'GAME OVER' if all levels completed) and 'Total Score', along with 'Retry Level' / 'Main Menu' buttons.\n    *   **Transitions From:** GamePlaying (ran out of time, too many mistakes, or completed all levels).\n    *   **Transitions To:** LevelSelect (via 'Retry Level' or 'Main Menu' button).\n6.  **Paused:**\n    *   Game state frozen, displays 'PAUSED' and 'Resume', 'Main Menu' buttons.\n    *   **Transitions From:** GamePlaying (via 'Escape' key or dedicated pause button).\n    *   **Transitions To:** GamePlaying (via 'Resume' button), MainMenu (via 'Main Menu' button).\n7.  **Options:**\n    *   Adjust sound volume, maybe display controls. \n    *   **Transitions From:** MainMenu.\n    *   **Transitions To:** MainMenu.\n8.  **HighScores:**\n    *   Displays a list of top scores, fetched from local storage.\n    *   **Transitions From:** MainMenu, LevelComplete, EndGame.\n    *   **Transitions To:** MainMenu.",
  "win_conditions": "A player wins a specific level by successfully answering a predefined percentage of questions (e.g., 70% or more) within the total time limit for that level, and without exceeding the maximum allowed incorrect answers (if any). The overall game is won by successfully completing all available levels.",
  "lose_conditions": "A player loses a specific level if they fail to meet the correct answer threshold (e.g., less than 70% correct), or if they run out of the total allotted time for the level before completing it, or if they accumulate too many incorrect answers (e.g., 3 incorrect answers) during the level session. There is no 'game over' for the entire game, only for individual levels, allowing players to retry. However, if a player completes all levels, an 'End Game' state will be shown (which can be seen as a 'win' condition for the entire game experience).",
  "scoring_system": "The game incorporates a detailed scoring system:\n\n*   **Correct Answer:** +100 points per question answered correctly.\n*   **Streak Bonus:** For every consecutive correct answer after the first, an additional +10 points are awarded (e.g., 1st correct: 100, 2nd: 110, 3rd: 120, etc.). Streak resets on an incorrect answer.\n*   **Time Bonus:** If a question is answered within the first 3 seconds of its timer: +50 bonus points.\n*   **Level Completion Bonus:** +500 points upon successfully completing a level.\n*   **Hint Penalty:** Using a hint deducts 100 points from the current score.\n\n**High Score System:** The game will maintain a local high score list (top 5 scores) using `localStorage`. Scores are accumulated across all successfully completed levels within a single play session until a 'game over' (completion of all levels or player quits). \n\n**Score Display:** The current score will always be prominently displayed in the **top-right corner** of the screen, formatted as 'SCORE: [Current Score]'. When a bonus is awarded (e.g., +100), it should briefly pop up near the score display or the answered question.",
  "level_system": "The game will feature 5 distinct levels, each focusing on a different vocabulary set and mini-game type, with increasing difficulty:\n\n*   **Level Indicator:** A 'LEVEL: [Current Level Number]' display will be shown in the **top-left corner** of the screen during gameplay.\n*   **Advancement:** Players advance to the next level upon successfully completing the current one. A 'LEVEL COMPLETE!' screen will display the level score, overall score, and a 'Next Level' button.\n\n1.  **Level 1: Basic Nouns - Image to Word Matching**\n    *   **Difficulty:** Easy.\n    *   **Vocabulary:** 10 simple, common nouns (e.g., 'apple', 'cat', 'house', 'tree', 'car').\n    *   **Mini-game:** Image-to-Word Matching. Player sees an image and 4 word options. Selects the correct word.\n    *   **Questions:** 8 questions.\n    *   **Time per Question:** 15 seconds.\n    *   **Win Condition:** At least 6 correct answers.\n    *   **Progression:** Introduces core gameplay, clear images, basic words.\n\n2.  **Level 2: Basic Verbs - Multiple Choice Definition**\n    *   **Difficulty:** Moderate.\n    *   **Vocabulary:** 12 common verbs (e.g., 'run', 'jump', 'eat', 'sleep', 'read', 'write').\n    *   **Mini-game:** Multiple Choice Definition. Player sees a word and 4 definitions. Selects the correct definition.\n    *   **Questions:** 10 questions.\n    *   **Time per Question:** 12 seconds.\n    *   **Win Condition:** At least 7 correct answers.\n    *   **Progression:** More questions, slightly less time, introduces abstract definitions, requiring more cognitive effort than image recognition.\n\n3.  **Level 3: Adjectives & Adverbs - Typing Game**\n    *   **Difficulty:** Medium-Hard.\n    *   **Vocabulary:** 15 common adjectives and adverbs (e.g., 'happy', 'quickly', 'beautiful', 'slowly', 'tall', 'loudly').\n    *   **Mini-game:** Spelling/Typing. Player sees an image or definition/sentence clue and types the correct word. A virtual keyboard is displayed.\n    *   **Questions:** 12 questions.\n    *   **Time per Question:** 20 seconds.\n    *   **Win Condition:** At least 9 correct answers.\n    *   **Progression:** Requires precise spelling and recall, longer words, introduces typing mechanic, which is more challenging.\n\n4.  **Level 4: Intermediate Nouns - Word to Image Matching (Reversed)**\n    *   **Difficulty:** Hard.\n    *   **Vocabulary:** 15 moderately complex nouns (e.g., 'bicycle', 'instrument', 'mountain', 'library', 'concept', 'universe').\n    *   **Mini-game:** Word-to-Image Matching (reversed). Player sees a word and 4 image options. Selects the correct image.\n    *   **Questions:** 12 questions.\n    *   **Time per Question:** 10 seconds.\n    *   **Win Condition:** At least 9 correct answers.\n    *   **Progression:** More abstract nouns, shorter time limit per question, demands quicker recognition and decision-making.\n\n5.  **Level 5: Phrasal Verbs - Sentence Completion**\n    *   **Difficulty:** Very Hard.\n    *   **Vocabulary:** 10 common phrasal verbs (e.g., 'turn off', 'look up', 'give up', 'break down', 'call off').\n    *   **Mini-game:** Sentence Completion. Player sees a sentence with a blank and 4 phrasal verb options. Selects the one that best completes the sentence grammatically and contextually.\n    *   **Questions:** 10 questions.\n    *   **Time per Question:** 15 seconds.\n    *   **Win Condition:** At least 8 correct answers.\n    *   **Progression:** Most complex vocabulary, requires understanding of context and nuance, longer reading time, highest cognitive load.",
  "simplifications": "To streamline implementation for an HTML5 p5.js game, the following features from the original concept will be simplified:\n\n*   **In-game Currency & Customization:** The separate 'in-game currency' for customizing Momo's appearance or purchasing hints will be simplified. Instead, points earned directly contribute to the score, and hints can be 'bought' by deducting points from the current score (e.g., 100 points per hint). Momo will remain a static, friendly mascot without customizable features.\n*   **Lesson Packs & Unlocking:** The concept of 'themed lesson packs' and unlocking new categories will be abstracted into a linear 'level' progression. Completing Level N automatically unlocks Level N+1. There will be no complex branching lesson trees or separate 'packs' to manage.\n*   **Comprehensive Vocabulary Mastery Tracking:** The detailed system of 'systematically expand English vocabulary knowledge and achieve mastery' will be simplified to per-level success/failure. There will be no persistent tracking of individual word mastery across sessions.\n*   **Multiple Mini-games per Session:** Each level will focus on a single, specific mini-game type for consistency and simpler implementation, rather than potentially mixing multiple types within a single 1-2 minute session.\n*   **Multiplayer Features:** The original concept doesn't explicitly mention multiplayer, but any potential interpretation of 'social learning' or 'leaderboards beyond local' would be simplified to a single-player experience with local high scores only.\n*   **Hints Complexity:** Hints will be simple (e.g., for multiple choice, remove one incorrect option; for typing, reveal one letter), rather than a complex 'purchase helpful hints' system.",
  "implementation_notes": "The game will be built using p5.js, leveraging its drawing, event handling, and state management capabilities. Matter.js is not necessary as there are no physics-based interactions required. \n\n*   **Data Structures:** Vocabulary data (words, definitions, image URLs, options, correct answers) for each level should be stored in easily parsable JSON objects or arrays, loaded at the start of each level.\n*   **Game Loop:** The `draw()` function in p5.js will be used to manage game states and render elements accordingly.\n*   **Input Handling:** `keyPressed()` and `mouseClicked()` functions will be crucial for managing player input, distinguishing between typing input and UI interaction.\n*   **Responsive Design:** The p5.js canvas should be designed to scale appropriately or maintain a fixed aspect ratio for optimal viewing on various screen sizes (desktop/mobile web).\n*   **Local Storage:** `localStorage` will be used to persist high scores and potentially unlocked level status between browser sessions.\n*   **Sound Effects:** Implement simple sound effects for correct/incorrect answers, button clicks, and level transitions to enhance feedback.\n*   **User Interface:** Prioritize clear, intuitive UI design, ensuring clickable areas are sufficiently large for touch interactions, and keyboard shortcuts are clearly indicated where applicable.\n*   **Score & Level Display:** As specified, the score must be in the top-right and level indicator in the top-left, consistently throughout gameplay."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>
      <p id="gameControls" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_controls}</p>
    </div>
    <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/p5.js/1.4.0/p5.js"></script>
    <script src="https://unpkg.com/p5.collide2d@0.7.3/p5.collide2d.js"></script>
    <script type="module" src="game.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

</example_html>

Help Momo learn English vocabulary! Progress through 5 challenging levels, each focusing on different word types and mini-games. Match images to words, choose correct definitions, spell words by typing, and complete sentences. Earn points for correct answers, build streaks for bonus points, and race against the clock. Each level gets progressively harder with new vocabulary and game mechanics. Can you master all levels and achieve the highest score? ENTER: Start game from menu ARROW KEYS: Navigate menu options and highlight answer choices SPACE/ENTER: Confirm selection or submit answer KEYS 1-4: Directly select answer option in multiple choice KEYS A-Z: Type letters in spelling challenges BACKSPACE: Delete letter when typing SHIFT: Capitalize letters when typing ESC: Pause/unpause game R: Return to start screen from game over H: Use hint Momo's Word Quest

Help Momo learn English vocabulary! Progress through 5 challenging levels, each focusing on different word types and mini-games. Match images to words, choose correct definitions, spell words by typing, and complete sentences. Earn points for correct answers, build streaks for bonus points, and race against the clock. Each level gets progressively harder with new vocabulary and game mechanics. Can you master all levels and achieve the highest score?

ENTER: Start game from menu | ARROW KEYS: Navigate menu options and highlight answer choices | SPACE/ENTER: Confirm selection or submit answer | KEYS 1-4: Directly select answer option in multiple choice | KEYS A-Z: Type letters in spelling challenges | BACKSPACE: Delete letter when typing | SHIFT: Capitalize letters when typing | ESC: Pause/unpause game | R: Return to start screen from game over | H: Use hint (costs 100 points)
